title: Sharjah Archaeology Authority Opens World Heritage Office To Safeguard UNESCO Sites And Future Nominations
description: The Sharjah Archaeology Authority (SAA) has launched the Sharjah World Heritage Office to strengthen the management of World Heritage sites in the emirate and coordinate the protection of listed, nominated, and potential sites in line with international standards. The launch follows an Emiri Decree by His Highness Sheikh Dr Sultan bin Mohammed Al Qasimi, Supreme Council Member and Ruler of Sharjah, approving the authority’s organisational structure. It also coincides with the inscription of Faya on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2025, marking a more advanced phase in managing Sharjah’s cultural and natural heritage through an integrated framework covering research, nominations, documentation, and coordination with relevant entities. The office is responsible for developing and managing nomination files for sites of Outstanding Universal Value in the emirate, and for the administrative oversight of sites inscribed on the World Heritage List and the Tentative List. It also prepares digital databases, accurate records of cultural heritage sites, and specialised studies that support World Heritage work, strengthening monitoring, documentation, and protection efforts while enhancing the emirate’s readiness for future international nominations. In addition, the international AED2 million Faya Research Grant programme established by the Sharjah government for the Faya World Heritage Site will be [...]

title: Asia Pacific Banks’ Clean Energy Financing Still Lags Fossil Fuels: BloombergNEF
description: Major energy shocks in 2026 have again highlighted the risks that fossil-fuel dependency poses to global economies. This dependence is particularly acute in Asia Pacific markets outside China. Despite being one of the fastest-growing regions in Asia Pacific, investment and capital flows toward low-carbon energy supply are dwarfed by fossil-fuel import expenditures. Based on an evaluation of disclosures, BloombergNEF’s new report, Energy Supply Ratios for Investment and Financing in Asia , shows how investment and financing evolved across eight Asia Pacific economies. BloombergNEF noted that banks in the region are one of the key reasons behind the widening gap in clean energy investments, which have yet to outpace local fossil-fuel investments. Banks have struggled to scale low-carbon transactions relative to fossil-fuel deals, reflecting inertia in both the real economy and financing strategies. Financing ratio calculations and disclosures, already adopted by institutions including JPMorgan Chase , Citigroup , and Scotiabank , can provide visibility to investors and serve as valuable internal benchmarking and strategic tools. BloombergNEF’s findings also revealed that banks headquartered in Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, Malaysia , Thailand, Indonesia, and the Philippines facilitated 83 cents in clean energy supply for every dollar of fossil-fuel financing in 2024. Although this marked the highest level since 2021, it remained below the global ratio of 0.89:1. Fossil-Fuel Financing vs Low-Carbon Transactions The research found that banks across these markets facilitated around $240 billion in energy supply financing annually between 2022 and 2024. Fossil-fuel financing volumes declined gradually across these banks, while low-carbon volumes rose only marginally. Total low-carbon transactions in 2024 remained below both 2021 levels and fossil-fuel energy supply volumes. The report noted that the ratio between low-carbon and fossil-fuel energy supply volumes improved in 2023 and 2024 for banks from Japan , Taiwan, Malaysia, Thailand, and the Philippines when aggregated by market. Photograph: (BloombergNEF) It further found that institutions that increased their ratios in 2023 and 2024 included Japan’s Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group and Mizuho Financial Group , Taiwan’s Mega Financial Holding , Indonesia’s Bank Mandiri , Malaysia’s Maybank , and Thailand’s Krung Thai Bank and Kasikornbank . Standard Chartered , BNP Paribas , Groupe BPCE , and Deutsche Bank were among the leading banks from outside Asia Pacific facilitating energy supply financing in the Southeast Asian markets covered in the report. Photograph: (BloombergNEF) Power Grid & ESS Drive Low-Carbon Financing Growth Across low-carbon sectors, bank financing for wind and solar remained broadly flat between 2023 and 2024. However, power grids and energy storage systems (ESS) drove financing growth in 2024 across most markets. Large deals or issuers continued to significantly influence results. Financing for the largest solar and battery project in Southeast Asia boosted low-carbon volumes for banks in the Philippines. Banks in Thailand and Indonesia also saw fossil-fuel transactions decline as PTT and Pertamina had limited refinancing requirements. Domestic transactions tilted toward low-carbon activities for banks in five of the eight markets during 2023-2024, highlighting growing transition opportunities. Banks from Japan, for example, achieved a domestic ratio of 1.27:1, although this was offset by fossil-fuel deals in North America and Southeast Asia. Why Asia Pacific Firms Are Pivoting Toward Energy Transition Investment Energy transition investment grew 23% in 2025 across Asia Pacific markets excluding mainland China , well above the global growth rate of 8%. However, the investment ratio remained at just $1.3 in low-carbon energy supply for every dollar invested in fossil-fuel supply in 2025, still lagging behind Europe’s 3.5:1 ratio. This comparison excludes spending on fossil-fuel imports. South Korea, for instance, invested only 11 cents in local clean energy supply for every dollar spent on fossil-fuel imports. Globally, an average investment ratio of 4:1 between low-carbon and fossil-fuel energy supply is required throughout this decade to align with 1.5-degree warming scenarios relative to pre-industrial levels. Asia Pacific requires an even higher ratio to offset structurally lower investment in fossil fuel-producing markets.

title: Wall in Portugal restored using ancient technique
description: The southern wall of Alcácer do Sal Castle is being restored using rammed earth, an ancient technique that uses earth and lime, in an intervention that reveals the challenges of preserving a national monument with centuries of history.

title: Mysterious ancient tunnel discovered near Kibbutz Ramat Rachel
description: Archaeologists discover massive 50-meter underground passage during excavation for new Jerusalem neighborhood.
